Hello everybody and welcome to 7 figure emails installment number 6. This one is called the couples therapy. So if this is the first one you're jumping to for some reason, here's how these go down. First we're going to give a bit of a background on the template. Then we're going to do the persuasion deep dive. Really talking about what's at play here and why it works and why we need to be doing this. Third we'll break down the template itself including some questions you can ask to extract the content. And in the video below this one you should see an example. A walkthrough of how this actually played out across two launches actually. So that's how we're going to do it. A little bit of a background. Harry Metzali one of my favorite movies of all time. And if you just kind of watch how their relationship slowly degrades, it all comes down to you. The number one relationship killer in general which is complacency. Which is the number one relationship and sales killer is taking one another for granted or simply forgetting the reasons we fell in love in the first place. Or why our audience fell in love with us or needed us in the first place. So this is why all relationships end. This is why all sales don't happen. Maybe those are big over generalized statements. But for the most part, the true. If we forget why we fell in love or we're no longer doing the things that cause that relationship to blossom in the first place, things are going to fall apart. And same goes with your sales messages and your relationship with your audience. This is especially true and useful if there's been a significant lag between when your prospect originally signed up for your email list and the offer you're putting in front of them. Or if they've been receiving a ton of personality based content or content that sways from that initial core intent. Because when we create a broad mix of content or leverage our personalities in our brand, sometimes what happens is that our audience forgets the core implicit agreement in our relationship. And the core implicit agreement is really that first fundamental promise or desire or problem they needed to solve that got them on our list to begin with. That's what we want to return to. This is really the foundation of what our relationship with them as a buyer is. So we want to return to that. So for any relationship or sale to occur, there needs to be a clear recognizable value exchange. And if that value has been obscured either by time, too much time lapsing between the original sign up and when that offer is or content that doesn't always enforce that main value exchange and your authority or ability to deliver it, then it's really time to enter couples therapy and to remind your audience of that core implicit agreement that drives your relationship for. So at its very core, this template is about reminding your audience of their initial core intent. Because once upon a time they did take that action of signing up to your email list with a certain intent associated with it and encouraging them to remain consistent and committed to fulfilling that intent as well as your as well as reestablishing your authority and ability to deliver it, which once again, they believed in at the beginning of your relationship. So it's really a return to that core intent and your ability to fulfill it. That was really the foundation of your relationship to begin with. All right. So the persuasion deep dive, this is what's at play here. We're really enforcing that implied commitment and consistency, because if you're emailing them, once upon a time, they signed up to your list to either overcome a problem or achieve a result. We need them to reconnect with that and stay consistent with it. Next thing is reestablishing our authority. So if our messaging has been broad or personality based, yes, we've given value. Yes, we've built trust. Yes, we've been so many great things and maybe even bonded with our audience, but we've also diluted our authority or spread it broadly and diluted it away from what we are actually here to serve them with. So this template is about redensifying, taking all that authority and really compacting it back to that initial core promise or that initial core desire that had them signing up to our list to begin with, and that is associated with the offer we're putting in front of them. Persuasion principle number three is having them identify as a buyer via the perfect for you if section. So just like our prospect needs to re relate to us as an authority on the topic, they need to remember who they are within the context of this relationship. Who are they on the buyer side of this value exchange? And in order and in order to do that, we use the perfect for you if format, which some of you may be familiar with from previous lessons in 10x launches or 10x emails, but we will repeat it again here is this is all about coaching identification as a buyer by mirroring back the qualities or characteristics of a perfect buyer. So this is about reminding them of who they are within this value exchange. All right. So where to send it? This could be sent as an alternative to the perfect for you with template inside 10x launches or 10x emails. Once again, you'll want to send this as an alternative, especially if there has been a big time lapse between when they signed up and when they're seeing an offer or if it's been diluted across other products, other personality based content, you will want to send this one out to just reestablish the relationship around the exchange or the implied exchange that they signed up to begin with. You could also send this as a standalone sales email or as an opening day launch email to non registrants of a webinar. So all that said, message mining questions for the intro and the lead. What is the core intent or commitment that my perfect customer would have had upon signing up for my list? What did they want to achieve and what they want to avoid? So a moment of highest tension or the moment of highest pleasure. Why did they believe that I could uniquely help them with it? What might have been my perceived USP? So this is really about that initial core relationship. And then for the perfect for you if section, which is going to help them re-identify as a buyer, we could ask questions like how are they already investing time, energy, money and solving the problem or achieving the outcome? What's your product's ultimate outcome? What's your product's quickest, most valuable win? Not the ultimate thing they're going to achieve, but the quickest, most exciting thing that they might achieve within minutes or hours or a day of interacting with your product. What's your customer's minimum viable commitment to achieve it, which is usually sign up risk-free for 30 days or whatever it is. What action do they need to take in order to achieve it? Cool. So couples, therapy, theme plate template. Here's how it goes. So this is a bit of a long one. But once again, it takes a bit of work to re-establish the relationship in a way that is profitable and more congruent with the initial intent. So we're going to start off with that relational recap. You know, once upon a time you signed up for my list, it was probably for intention one, intention two, intention three. And these are all associated with either the problem they want help solving or the or the pleasure that they want to be connected with. Those are really the basis of those intentions. Then close the commitment loop with your product. It's essentially saying you signed up with these intentions. Here's our chance to actually make good on it. So that's your first call to action. And then you're going to transition to the perfect for you if section. And there are two ways to do this. And you'll see one in each example. You could presume that based off of that relational recap one through three, that they should have said yes to your product. Right. Like this was their intention. They now have a chance to make good on it. They didn't. So there must still be some fear or hesitance present. And then you could go into the perfect for you if or you could say, if you haven't, if this is the first opportunity they have to buy this product, then you say that this has been the moment they've been waiting for to make good on that commitment. You know, this is why they signed up in the first place. Now they actually have a chance to make good on that. And you're going to see how that played out in the DCA 19 example in the video below. And then perfect for you if section options. So this is loose. This is flexible. But essentially what you're doing with these seven to nine bullets or numbers, if you will, is reflecting back who your perfect buyer is. You really want to create identification with someone who would buy. So the way I like to do is I like to start with this list and then I'll make iterations. You know, if I feel called to, but really starting with this as the base. So the person just getting started, who wants to do it right the first time, the person already in it, struggling, who wants to finally fix it once and for all. Any other alternative avatars that you need to include in this sales message. A general desirable trait, something like being willing to put in the work, specific traits or actions that back it up. How do you prove that they're doing number four? What does it actually look like? Number six, a perfect buyer is obviously someone who's aware and excited about the big final outcome of your product. They're also aware and excited about the quickest, most valuable win they can experience with your product. These are really the core makeups of what makes a buyer. They have to be excited about these things. And then they're willing to take the minimum viable commitment to do it, which is usually going to be taking you up on your guarantee, clicking the link and going through the enrollment processor, the sales process, whatever that looks like. And then of course, they're also aware of any bonuses, discounts, perks of buying now as opposed to later. So these are flexible, but essentially you're painting the picture of someone who would be in a perfect situation to buy right now and then closing with a final call to action. So that is couples therapy. It's kind of like the perfect for you if email that you may have seen before, but really with this lead that is focused on reestablishing the balance of your relationship with your customer and why it even exists in the first place. So, yeah, that's it. We will see you in the next video where we break down two examples from two different launches. So I will see you there. Enjoy the John Qsack.
